title: God Is Faithful
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-10-24 -PM- God is Faithful - Psalm 31 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Psalm as Christ's Own
subpoints: Psalm 31 is the experience of David as representative believer
Jesus quotes verse 5 from the cross (Luke 23:46), making this Psalm His own
Even in the most excruciating suffering, God is reliably faithful and delivers His servant

outline.title: The Complexity of David's Suffering
subpoints: External suffering: enemies out to get him, hypervigilance and threat of attack (vv. 4, 13)
Internal and chronic suffering: life spent with grief, years with sighing (vv. 9-10)
Suffering comes from multiple sources: his own sin (v. 10), obedience to the Lord (vv. 6-8, 11-12), and the sins of others (v. 13)

outline.title: Why David Turns to God in Suffering
subpoints: God is his rock and fortress — the fortified city on the hill, impenetrable (v. 3)
God knows what he is going through — not like an automated phone service, but truly present (v. 7)
God has delivered him in the past and set his feet in a wide place (v. 8)
David trusts the Lord — God has kept all His covenant promises (v. 14)
God is good (v. 19), kind (v. 21), hears prayer (v. 22), and strengthens those who hope (v. 24)

outline.title: The Outcome: Deliverance
subpoints: Deliverance came in verses 21-22, even when David thought God had abandoned him
God never gives up on His people even when they are on the brink of abandoning their faith
David's experience becomes comfort for others: 'Be of good courage, and He shall strengthen your heart' (v. 24)

summary: In the evening service, Pastor Tito Lyro brings together the morning's theme of faithfulness with the adult Sunday school discussion on suffering by preaching Psalm 31 as David's testimony of God's reliable faithfulness in the midst of complex, chronic suffering. He begins by observing that Jesus appropriated this very Psalm from the cross (Luke 23:46), giving it the highest possible endorsement as a description of God's faithfulness even in the worst suffering imaginable. David's suffering in the Psalm is presented as genuinely complex — external threats, chronic inner grief, sources as varied as his own sin, his obedience to God, and the sins of others — reflecting the Bible's realistic treatment of suffering. Pastor Lyro then works through the reasons David turns to God rather than elsewhere: God is an impenetrable fortress, He knows precisely what is happening in David's life, He has delivered him in the past, and He is trustworthy, good, kind, attentive, faithful to the faithful, and a strengthener of those who hope in Him. The sermon closes with the observation that David's deliverance came when he was on the very brink of giving up, and that his experience is turned into an invitation for others: "Be of good courage, and He shall strengthen your heart, all you who hope in the Lord" (v. 24).
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 31

For our Scripture reading this afternoon, let us turn to the 31st Psalm. Psalm 31. We are going to bring together the idea of suffering that we looked at in Sunday school this morning with the idea of God's faithfulness that we've seen in the morning service.

Psalm 31. This is the word of our Lord:

"In You, O Lord, I put my trust; let me never be ashamed. Deliver me in Your righteousness. Bow down Your ear to me, deliver me speedily; be my rock of refuge, a fortress of defense to save me. For You are my rock and my fortress; therefore, for Your name's sake, lead me and guide me. Pull me out of the net which they have secretly laid for me, for You are my strength. Into Your hand I commit my spirit; You have redeemed me, O Lord God of truth.

"I have hated those who regard useless idols; but I trust in the Lord. I will be glad and rejoice in Your mercy, for You have considered my trouble; You have known my soul in adversities, and have not shut me up into the hand of the enemy. You have set my feet in a wide place.

"Have mercy on me, O Lord, for I am in trouble; my eye wastes away with grief, yes, my soul and my body. For my life is spent with grief, and my years with sighing; my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away. I am a reproach among all my enemies, but especially among my neighbors, and am repulsive to my acquaintances; those who see me outside flee from me. I am forgotten like a dead man, out of mind; I am like a broken vessel. For I hear the slander of many; fear is on every side; while they take counsel together against me, they scheme to take away my life.

"But as for me, I trust in You, O Lord; I say, 'You are my God.' My times are in Your hand; deliver me from the hand of my enemies, and from those who persecute me. Make Your face shine upon Your servant; save me for Your mercies' sake. Do not let me be ashamed, O Lord, for I have called upon You; let the wicked be ashamed; let them be silent in the grave. Let the lying lips be put to silence, which speak insolent things proudly and contemptuously against the righteous.

"Oh, how great is Your goodness, which You have laid up for those who fear You, which You have prepared for those who trust in You in the presence of the sons of men! You shall hide them in the secret place of Your presence from the plots of man; You shall keep them secretly in a pavilion from the strife of tongues. Blessed be the Lord, for He has shown me His marvelous kindness in a strong city!

"For I said in my haste, 'I am cut off from before Your eyes'; nevertheless You heard the voice of my supplications when I cried out to You. Oh, love the Lord, all you His saints! For the Lord preserves the faithful, and fully repays the proud person. Be of good courage, and He shall strengthen your heart, all you who hope in the Lord."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Glorious God, thank you for your Word. We pray that you speak to us through it this afternoon. In Jesus' name. Amen.

## Introduction

This Psalm, as is the case with many Psalms, is the result of suffering in the life of the believer — here represented by David as the quintessential representative believer. We often read the Bible thinking that it is not about our reality. We often think the Bible speaks of suffering in general but not to our specific situation.

I hope that Psalm 31 disabuses us of these ideas, because the Bible speaks to all kinds of suffering, including whatever specific suffering you might be going through.

The theme of this Psalm is that God is reliably faithful, and because of that, He can be trusted to deliver His servant.

Jesus appropriated the Psalm to Himself — especially verse 5 — when He cried from the cross in Luke 23:46: "Father, into Your hands I commit my spirit." Jesus understood that God remained faithful to Him even in the midst of excruciating suffering, and that God was going to deliver Him even through death. That is the highest possible endorsement of this Psalm as a description of God's faithfulness in the worst suffering imaginable.

## The Suffering of David

Let's take a look at the suffering of David and see that the Bible knows all about suffering.

In verse 4, he is suffering at the hands of his enemies. People are out to get him — the idea of having to be hypervigilant because attack can come at any time.

But in verses 9 and 10, we also see that his suffering is not only external but internal and chronic. He speaks about "life spent with grief, years with sighing." David is acquainted with long-term suffering — not just a casual sufferer.

Notice the honest assessment of David concerning his sin in the midst of suffering. In verse 10, he says part of his suffering is there because of his own sin. But also because of his obedience to the Lord — in verses 6-8 and 11-12, his enemies are upset with him because he is obedient to the Lord. And some suffering comes from the sins of others — verse 13.

This is instructive: suffering is usually complex. It cannot be explained by just one factor. There are multiple sources, and David sees them all clearly.

## Why David Turns to God

Why is David turning to God for deliverance? What is it that he knows about God that causes him to look to the Lord in this time of chronic suffering?

In verse 3 he says God is his rock and his fortress — like the impenetrable Jewish fortresses on hilltops, like Masada. He seeks help from God in suffering because God is his rock and fortress.

He also says that God knows what is going on with him (v. 7). God is not like those automated phone services where you have to repeat your whole problem multiple times. God knows what David is going through. Even before David tells Him, Jesus knows what David is going through — as He made this Psalm His own on the cross.

David goes to God for relief because God has delivered him in the past. In verse 8, "You have set my feet in a wide place." Not a narrow pole to balance on, but a wide, firm place. Not removal from suffering but a place where you can withstand it — both feet firmly grounded.

David trusts in the Lord (v. 14): "But as for me, I trust in You, O Lord. You are my God." God has shown Himself to be faithful to David. He has kept all His covenant promises.

He goes to God because God is good (v. 19), kind (v. 21), hears prayer (v. 22), is faithful to the faithful (v. 24), and strengthens those who hope in Him (v. 24).

And because David knows God will vindicate His people (vv. 19-20) — sometimes in this life, always in the life to come. "Therefore I can trust in Him and I can go on with life, loving the people around me."

## The Outcome: Deliverance

What is the outcome of David's cry to the Lord? Verses 21-22 tell us: deliverance. It is interesting that when you look at those verses, the deliverance did not come until he almost disappears — because at one point he thought God had not heard his prayer. He was on the brink of abandoning his faith. He thought that because the suffering had not stopped, God had abandoned him. But God never gives up on His people, even when we are about to give up on God.

And David's experience is turned into comfort and blessing for others. As the Psalm concludes in verse 24, David says: "Be of good courage, and He shall strengthen your heart, all you who hope in the Lord." I have tasted God and He has come through for me. You people — be comforted by my experience.

The Apostle Paul says in 1 Corinthians 10:13: "No temptation has overtaken you except such as is common to man; but God is faithful, who will not allow you to be tempted beyond what you are able, but with the temptation will also make the way of escape, that you may be able to bear it."

That is the message of the Psalm. Suffering is real. And so is what the Bible says about it. Suffering drives us — surprised, like David — to find deliverance in God, even if the suffering is not removed.

May we learn from the suffering of our brother David that we too may be comforted by our God who is near to those who suffer.

Let us pray. Father, thank you for your Word. Thank you for this Psalm. We pray that we would grow even in the midst of suffering, knowing that you are near us and that we can go to you for deliverance, that you never abandon us, that you are faithful to us. In Jesus' name. Amen.
